topic: A person is asked to give a speech at their parent's retirement party
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation arrived with the usual fanfare, a thick card promising a "celebration of a lifetime" for my parents' retirement. I stared at the embossed lettering, a slow, heavy sigh escaping my lips. A speech. Of course. As if I possessed some innate, dazzling eloquence. I pinched the bridge of my nose, the skin protesting the pressure. When the family gathered for the party, the forced cheerfulness of everyone seemed to vibrate painfully in the air. I stood at the podium, the microphone feeling cold against my palm. The opening remarks were a blur of strained smiles and overly sentimental anecdotes. Every time someone clapped too loudly, I subtly shifted my weight, a barely perceptible tightening in my jaw. I kept my gaze fixed on the notes, the words feeling like sandpaper in my mouth, determined only to get through the required duration with the utmost minimal expenditure of energy.

[story 2]

"Oh, you're up next!" Aunt Carol chirped, practically vibrating with excitement as she handed me the microphone. I took it with a stiff, deliberate movement, my fingers brushing hers briefly before I retreated to the side of the stage. The room was too bright, the background music offensively upbeat. My internal monologue was a low, continuous stream of complaints: *This is tedious. Why me?* I scanned the faces—the relatives, the friends—each expression a carefully calibrated mask of warmth. When I started speaking, my voice came out unnaturally flat, a monotone delivery that felt alien even to my own ears. I focused intently on the floorboards, willing them to somehow absorb the excess noise. I wanted to be anywhere else, doing anything but standing there, reciting platitudes about legacy and future plans.

[story 3]

I watched my mother, beaming beside my father, a picture of serene contentment. It was exhausting just observing the manufactured joy. When the host finally called my name, I walked to the stage with an air of practiced resignation. The spotlight felt like a physical weight pressing down on my shoulders. As I began to speak, I consciously slowed my pace, ensuring every word was articulated with precise, almost mechanical clarity. I noticed a waiter hovering near the edge of the stage, and for a fleeting second, I wished he would simply vanish. My attention drifted to a smudge on the podium, a tiny imperfection that seemed infinitely more interesting than the outpouring of generalized gratitude expected of me. I finished quickly, the applause sounding distant and hollow, and immediately sought the nearest exit.
